name: competitor-analysis
description: Analyze competitor website structures, messaging patterns, and unique features. Use when researching competition, identifying gaps, or informing content strategy.
capabilities: Analyze competitor website page structures including hero sections, value propositions, and CTAs
Extract headlines, messaging patterns, and trust signals from competitor pages
Identify unique features and interactive elements that differentiate competitors
Perform systematic gap analysis to identify opportunities for differentiation
Rank competitors by market presence (national, regional, local, emerging)
Generate structured JSON reports with competitor insights for strategic planning
author: CleanExpo
securitySummary: All 13 static findings evaluated as false positives. The skill is a legitimate business research tool that uses standard web scraping tools to analyze competitor websites. Detected patterns (URLs in metadata, backticks in markdown, text patterns flagged as cryptographic) are benign documentation and configuration elements with no security implications.
valueStatement: Understanding competitor positioning is essential for strategic business decisions. This skill provides a systematic framework to analyze competitor websites, extract actionable insights, and identify market opportunities.
userTitle: Analyze competitor websites for strategic insights
limitations: Analysis is limited to publicly accessible website content only
Cannot access password-protected pages, login areas, or gated content
Results reflect website content at the time of analysis and may change
Generated insights should be verified against current competitor offerings

useCases.title: Pre-redesign competitive research
useCases.description: Analyze competitor websites before a website redesign project to understand industry standards, identify best practices, and find differentiation opportunities.

useCases.title: Market entry strategy
useCases.description: Research existing competitors when entering a new market or geographic region to understand local competition, pricing models, and messaging strategies.

useCases.title: Quarterly competitive review
useCases.description: Conduct regular competitive analysis to track competitor changes, new feature launches, and messaging shifts over time.

promptTemplates.title: Quick competitor snapshot
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze [competitor website URL] for their homepage structure, main value propositions, and key unique features. List their primary CTAs and trust signals.
promptTemplates.scenario: Getting a quick overview of a competitor

promptTemplates.title: Detailed page-by-page analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Perform a complete analysis of [competitor URL]. Cover their homepage, about page, services, and pricing pages. Extract section order, headlines, CTAs, and trust signals from each.
promptTemplates.scenario: Deep dive into a specific competitor

promptTemplates.title: Multi-competitor comparison
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze these three competitors: [URL 1], [URL 2], [URL 3]. Compare their messaging patterns, unique features, and trust signals. Identify gaps in the market that none address.
promptTemplates.scenario: Comparing multiple competitors for strategic gaps

promptTemplates.title: Gap analysis report
promptTemplates.prompt: After analyzing [list competitor URLs], create a gap analysis report. Identify features, trust signals, and messaging themes that competitors have that we lack. Prioritize recommendations by potential impact.

bestPractices: Analyze patterns, not content. Focus on why competitor structures work rather than copying their specific text or designs.
Document analysis dates. Websites change frequently, so record when you analyzed each competitor for future reference.
Prioritize insights by strategic impact. Not all gaps are equally valuable—focus on opportunities that align with your strengths.
antiPatterns: Copying competitor content directly. This raises legal and ethical issues and misses the point of competitive analysis.
Analyzing only direct competitors. Emerging players and adjacent market entrants often reveal bigger disruption risks.
Treating analysis as a one-time activity. Markets evolve continuously, so competitive analysis should be an ongoing practice.

faq.question: What websites can this skill analyze?
faq.answer: The skill can analyze any publicly accessible website. It cannot access password-protected pages, login areas, admin dashboards, or content behind paywalls.

faq.question: How detailed is the analysis?
faq.answer: The analysis covers page structure, headlines, CTAs, trust signals, unique features, and messaging patterns. Each page type (homepage, about, services, pricing) has a specific framework for evaluation.

faq.question: Can I use this for international competitors?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill works with any website regardless of geographic location. Consider language differences if analyzing non-English sites.

faq.question: How often should I run competitor analysis?
faq.answer: For fast-moving markets, quarterly analysis is recommended. For stable markets, bi-annual updates may suffice. Always analyze after major competitor announcements or product launches.

faq.question: Does this skill track competitors over time?
faq.answer: The skill provides point-in-time analysis. For historical tracking, save your analysis reports and compare them against new analyses periodically.

faq.question: What tools does this skill use?
faq.answer: The skill uses web browsing tools (Playwright, Exa) to navigate and extract content from competitor websites. No local scripts or command execution is involved.
